microsoft-office.title: Microsoft Office
microsoft-office.bestMethod: Use Office on the web (office.com) in a browser, or switch to LibreOffice / OnlyOffice for offline desktop editing.
whatBreaks: No native Linux desktop suite; Wine installs of the full suite are unreliable and unsupported
VBA macros and complex add-ins do not run in LibreOffice
Advanced formatting and co-authoring features are limited in the web version offline
microsoft-office.notes: Microsoft ships no Linux desktop build. Web Office is the only first-party path; OnlyOffice has the best OOXML fidelity among native alternatives in 2026.

slack.title: Slack
slack.bestMethod: Install the official Slack Linux app (deb/rpm or Flathub com.slack.Slack).
whatBreaks: Occasional screen-share/Wayland quirks under some compositors
Global keyboard shortcuts can need extra configuration
slack.notes: First-party Linux app, near-identical to Windows.

zoom.title: Zoom
zoom.bestMethod: Install the official Zoom Linux client (deb/rpm or Flathub us.zoom.Zoom).
whatBreaks: Screen sharing under Wayland may need PipeWire portals enabled
A few advanced webinar/host controls lag the Windows build
zoom.notes: Official Linux client is well maintained.

discord.title: Discord
discord.bestMethod: Install the official Discord Linux app (deb or Flathub com.discordapp.Discord).
whatBreaks: Native screen-share with audio needs Wayland/PipeWire setup
The deb sometimes lags the auto-updater; Flatpak updates more smoothly
discord.notes: Fully native; Vesktop is a popular community client with better Linux screen-share audio.

visual-studio.title: Visual Studio (IDE)
visual-studio.bestMethod: Use a Windows VM for full Visual Studio, or switch to VS Code / JetBrains Rider, both native on Linux, for .NET work.
whatBreaks: No Linux version of the full Visual Studio IDE
Some Windows-only designers (WinForms/WPF visual designers) have no Linux equal
MSVC-specific build setups differ from .NET on Linux
visual-studio.notes: .NET itself runs great on Linux; Microsoft confirmed (Sept 2025) no plans for a Linux VS IDE, so the full IDE and Windows designers force a VM.

figma.title: Figma
figma.bestMethod: Use Figma in the browser (full-featured), or the community Figma-Linux desktop wrapper for an app-like experience.
whatBreaks: No official Linux desktop app (Figma-Linux is community-built)
Local font sync needs the community font helper
Offline editing is limited
figma.notes: Figma is web-first; the community Figma-Linux (Flathub io.github.Figma_Linux.figma_linux) provides a desktop window.

canva.title: Canva
canva.bestMethod: Use Canva in the browser (it is web-first); optionally install it as a PWA.
whatBreaks: No native Linux desktop app (works fully in-browser anyway)
Offline use is limited
canva.notes: Canva is a web app first, so Linux is a non-issue.

davinci-resolve.title: DaVinci Resolve
davinci-resolve.bestMethod: Download the official Blackmagic Design Linux installer; use the studio install scripts (e.g. MakeResolveDeb) on Debian/Ubuntu.
whatBreaks: Linux build officially supports CentOS/RHEL/Rocky; other distros need the community repackaging script
H.264/H.265 consumer codecs need transcoding or the Studio (paid) version
Requires a supported NVIDIA/AMD GPU with proper drivers
davinci-resolve.notes: Blackmagic ships a genuine native Linux build of Resolve 21; codec licensing (not the OS) is the main friction.

obs-studio.title: OBS Studio
obs-studio.bestMethod: Install the official OBS Studio Linux build (PPA/repo or Flathub com.obsproject.Studio).
whatBreaks: Some capture plugins are Windows-only
Game capture works differently on Linux (use PipeWire window/screen capture)
obs-studio.notes: Fully native and first-class on Linux.

affinity-photo.title: Affinity Photo
affinity-photo.bestMethod: Install via the community ElementalWarrior Wine build / rum + Affinity script; expect setup effort and occasional breakage.
whatBreaks: No official Linux support from Serif
Requires a patched Wine build; updates can break the install
GPU acceleration and some filters may be unstable
Hardware (Wacom) pressure can need extra configuration
affinity-photo.notes: The Affinity-on-Linux community script (ElementalWarrior fork) makes v2 usable for many, but it is unofficial and fragile.

quickbooks.title: QuickBooks
quickbooks.bestMethod: Use QuickBooks Online in a browser; for QuickBooks Desktop use a Windows VM or migrate the company file to QBO.
whatBreaks: No Linux desktop version
QuickBooks Desktop company files (.QBW) need a Windows VM or QBO migration
Some payroll/tax add-ons are Windows-only
quickbooks.notes: QBO is the clean Linux path; Desktop users must migrate or virtualize.

autocad.title: AutoCAD
autocad.bestMethod: Use AutoCAD web for light edits, a Windows VM for full desktop AutoCAD, or switch to LibreCAD / BricsCAD (native Linux).
whatBreaks: No native Linux AutoCAD; Wine is partial and unsupported
AutoCAD web lacks 3D and many advanced commands
AutoLISP/plugins and Xrefs workflows do not move to LibreCAD
autocad.notes: BricsCAD offers a native Linux DWG-compatible commercial option; AutoCAD itself stays on VM/web.

spotify.title: Spotify
spotify.bestMethod: Install the official Spotify Linux client (snap or Flathub com.spotify.Client), or use the web player.
whatBreaks: The Linux client lags slightly behind Windows on some new features
Local-file playback can need extra configuration
spotify.notes: Official Linux desktop client plus full web player.

notepad-plus-plus.title: Notepad++
notepad-plus-plus.bestMethod: Run Notepad++ under Wine/Bottles (rated Gold), or switch to a native editor like Kate, gedit, or VS Code.
whatBreaks: No native Linux build
Some Notepad++ plugins misbehave under Wine
Windows-style file associations need manual setup
notepad-plus-plus.notes: Notepad++ runs well under Wine, but most users simply adopt Kate or VS Code natively.

paint-net.title: Paint.NET
paint-net.bestMethod: Use a native editor (Krita, GIMP, or Pinta, which clones the Paint.NET UI); Paint.NET itself only runs under Wine with effort.
whatBreaks: No native Linux build
The .NET dependency makes Wine installs finicky
Paint.NET plugins (.dll effects) generally do not load under Wine
paint-net.notes: Pinta is a near drop-in clone of Paint.NET and the recommended replacement.

dropbox.title: Dropbox
dropbox.bestMethod: Install the official Dropbox Linux client (deb/rpm/tarball); it provides folder sync and a tray icon.
whatBreaks: Official client only supports unencrypted ext4 home folders (encrypted/btrfs/zfs setups are unsupported)
Smart Sync / online-only files are limited on Linux
Tray-icon integration needs an app-indicator extension on GNOME
dropbox.notes: Official Linux client exists but has a long-standing filesystem restriction; rclone/Insync cover edge cases.

google-drive.title: Google Drive
google-drive.bestMethod: Use Drive in the browser, the built-in GNOME Online Accounts integration (Files/Nautilus), or rclone for folder sync.
whatBreaks: No official 'Drive for desktop' client on Linux
GNOME Online Accounts mount can be slow and lacks offline files
Real-time co-editing requires the browser
google-drive.notes: Google ships no Linux desktop client; GNOME integration or rclone fill the gap well.

github-desktop.title: GitHub Desktop
github-desktop.bestMethod: Use the community 'shiftkey/desktop' Linux fork of GitHub Desktop (deb/rpm/Flatpak), or a native Git GUI like GitKraken or git-cola.
whatBreaks: No official GitHub Desktop build for Linux (only a community fork)
The fork can lag upstream releases
Some GitHub-specific auth flows differ from the official app
github-desktop.notes: GitHub never shipped an official Linux client; the shiftkey fork is the de-facto Linux build.

whatsapp-desktop.title: WhatsApp Desktop
whatsapp-desktop.bestMethod: Use WhatsApp Web in a browser, or a community Electron wrapper (ZapZap / WhatsApp for Linux / Flathub).
whatBreaks: No official Linux desktop app
Community wrappers can break when WhatsApp Web changes
Voice/video calls are not supported via WhatsApp Web
whatsapp-desktop.notes: WhatsApp Web covers messaging; calling still requires a phone or other app.

microsoft-365.title: Microsoft 365
microsoft-365.bestMethod: Access Microsoft 365 apps through office.com / outlook.com in any browser; pair with OneDrive web for storage.
whatBreaks: No Linux desktop apps; everything runs in the browser
Offline desktop editing requires switching to LibreOffice/OnlyOffice
Some enterprise add-ins and Teams integrations are Windows/Mac only
microsoft-365.notes: M365 subscription works fully via web on Linux; the desktop client install is not available for Linux.

microsoft-word.title: Microsoft Word
microsoft-word.bestMethod: Word on the web for fidelity-critical documents; LibreOffice Writer or OnlyOffice Document Editor for offline use.
whatBreaks: Macros (VBA) and document automation do not run under LibreOffice
Complex track-changes and mail-merge workflows can differ in alternatives
Some fonts and layout reflow slightly when round-tripping DOCX
microsoft-word.notes: Everyday documents migrate cleanly; heavy VBA/mail-merge users should test first.

microsoft-excel.title: Microsoft Excel
microsoft-excel.bestMethod: Excel on the web covers most needs; LibreOffice Calc or OnlyOffice for offline spreadsheets, accepting macro/add-in limits.
whatBreaks: VBA macros are unreliable or non-functional under LibreOffice Calc
Power Query / Power Pivot data models are not supported in alternatives
Third-party Excel add-ins (e.g. Solver variants, financial plugins) do not load
Some complex array/dynamic-array functions behave differently
microsoft-excel.notes: The single biggest Office migration blocker. Power-user spreadsheets with VBA/Power Query should stay on Excel Web or a Windows VM.

microsoft-powerpoint.title: Microsoft PowerPoint
microsoft-powerpoint.bestMethod: PowerPoint on the web preserves animations best; LibreOffice Impress or OnlyOffice for offline editing.
whatBreaks: Complex animations and slide transitions can render incorrectly in LibreOffice Impress
Embedded media and morph transitions may not survive round-trips
Designer/AI layout features are not available offline
microsoft-powerpoint.notes: Simple decks migrate fine; animation-heavy presentations are best kept in PowerPoint Web.

microsoft-outlook.title: Microsoft Outlook
microsoft-outlook.bestMethod: Use Outlook on the web (outlook.com / OWA), or connect the account to Thunderbird/Evolution via IMAP/Exchange.
whatBreaks: No native Linux Outlook client
Exchange calendar/contacts sync in Thunderbird needs the TbSync/EWS add-on and is imperfect
Some corporate Exchange features (rules, shared mailboxes) are limited in third-party clients
microsoft-outlook.notes: OWA is full-featured; Evolution has the best native Exchange/EWS support of the desktop options.

microsoft-access.title: Microsoft Access
microsoft-access.bestMethod: Run Access in a Windows VM, or migrate the database to LibreOffice Base / a real RDBMS (PostgreSQL, SQLite).
whatBreaks: No Linux version and no web version
Access forms, macros and VBA do not port to LibreOffice Base cleanly
ACCDB applications often need a full rebuild on another platform
microsoft-access.notes: One of the hardest Office apps to leave behind; departmental Access apps usually require a VM or a rewrite.

microsoft-onenote.title: Microsoft OneNote
microsoft-onenote.bestMethod: Use OneNote on the web (onenote.com), or migrate notes to Joplin / Obsidian for a native experience.
whatBreaks: No native Linux OneNote client
Pen/ink and some embedded-object features are limited in the web app
Migrating existing notebooks to alternatives requires export tooling
microsoft-onenote.notes: Web OneNote is solid; many users move to Joplin for offline/native note-taking.

onedrive.title: OneDrive
onedrive.bestMethod: Use the OneDrive web interface, or the community 'abraunegg/onedrive' CLI client for real folder sync; Insync offers a paid GUI.
whatBreaks: No official Linux OneDrive client
Files On-Demand / placeholder files are not supported by community clients
Sharing and co-authoring still route through the web
onedrive.notes: The abraunegg CLI client is reliable for full sync; GUI users often pay for Insync or move to Nextcloud.

microsoft-visio.title: Microsoft Visio
microsoft-visio.bestMethod: Use Visio for the web (with a plan), or switch to draw.io / LibreOffice Draw for diagramming on Linux.
whatBreaks: No Linux desktop version
Web Visio requires a separate paid plan and has reduced features
VSDX fidelity in draw.io/LibreOffice Draw is imperfect for complex stencils
microsoft-visio.notes: draw.io imports VSDX reasonably and is the most popular Linux replacement.

microsoft-project.title: Microsoft Project
microsoft-project.bestMethod: Use Project for the web (paid plan), or migrate to ProjectLibre / GanttProject for native Linux scheduling.
whatBreaks: No Linux desktop client
MPP file fidelity in ProjectLibre is partial
Enterprise resource-management and Project Online integrations are not replicated by alternatives
microsoft-project.notes: ProjectLibre is the closest free desktop replacement and reads many MPP files.

microsoft-teams.title: Microsoft Teams
microsoft-teams.bestMethod: Install Teams as a PWA from Edge/Chrome, or use the full web app at teams.microsoft.com; the native Linux client was retired.
whatBreaks: The native Linux Teams desktop client was discontinued (December 2022); only the PWA/web remains
Background blur and some advanced meeting effects are limited via PWA
Deep OS integration (some screen-share modes) can be less reliable than on Windows
microsoft-teams.notes: Microsoft retired the standalone Linux client; the PWA is the supported path and works for chat, calls and meetings.

citations.title: Teams progressive web app on Linux

skype.title: Skype
skype.bestMethod: Skype was retired by Microsoft in May 2025; migrate to Microsoft Teams (free) or another VoIP app.
whatBreaks: Skype is discontinued as a consumer product
Existing contacts/chats were migrated to Teams
No supported Linux client remains
skype.notes: Microsoft shut down Skype in May 2025 and pushed users to Teams; treat Skype itself as dead on every OS.

webex.title: Cisco Webex
webex.bestMethod: Install the official Webex Linux client (deb/rpm), or join meetings via the web app.
whatBreaks: Some enterprise device/calling features lag the Windows client
Screen sharing may require Wayland/PipeWire portals
webex.notes: Cisco maintains an official Linux build.

adobe-photoshop.title: Adobe Photoshop
adobe-photoshop.bestMethod: For serious work use a Windows VM; otherwise try Photoshop on the web, or switch to GIMP/Krita/Photopea. Community Wine patches (PhialsBasement, via Bottles/Lutris) can run PS 2021/2025 but are unofficial and fragile.
whatBreaks: No official Linux build; community Wine installs are unstable and break on updates
Camera Raw, generative AI features and many plugins fail under Wine
GPU-accelerated filters and color management are unreliable
Creative Cloud login/validation can fail under Wine
adobe-photoshop.notes: Photopea (web) is the closest drop-in for the UI; professionals dependent on plugins/Camera Raw should use a VM.

adobe-illustrator.title: Adobe Illustrator
adobe-illustrator.bestMethod: Use a Windows VM for production work, or switch to Inkscape / Affinity Designer; Illustrator on the web exists but is limited.
whatBreaks: No Linux build and Wine support is effectively broken
AI file fidelity in Inkscape is partial
Type, mesh gradients and plugins do not transfer cleanly
adobe-illustrator.notes: Inkscape covers most vector tasks; AI-format interchange is the main pain point.

adobe-lightroom.title: Adobe Lightroom
adobe-lightroom.bestMethod: Switch to Darktable or RawTherapee for native RAW workflow; use a Windows VM or Lightroom web if you must keep Adobe.
whatBreaks: No Linux version
Catalog/edit history does not import into Darktable
Adobe-specific lens profiles and AI masking are not replicated
adobe-lightroom.notes: Darktable is a genuinely strong non-destructive RAW editor and the standard Lightroom replacement on Linux.

adobe-indesign.title: Adobe InDesign
adobe-indesign.bestMethod: Use a Windows VM for production layouts, or migrate to Scribus / Affinity Publisher.
whatBreaks: No Linux version and no usable Wine path
INDD files cannot be opened by Scribus (only IDML, partially)
Typography, GREP styles and plugin ecosystem do not transfer
adobe-indesign.notes: Scribus is capable but workflow-different; serious DTP shops keep a Windows/Mac VM.

adobe-premiere-pro.title: Adobe Premiere Pro
adobe-premiere-pro.bestMethod: Switch to DaVinci Resolve (native Linux) or Kdenlive; use a Windows VM only if you must stay in Premiere.
whatBreaks: No Linux build; Wine does not run modern Premiere
PRPROJ projects do not import into other editors
Adobe Dynamic Link / After Effects round-tripping is lost
adobe-premiere-pro.notes: DaVinci Resolve runs natively on Linux and is a professional-grade replacement, easing this migration considerably.

adobe-after-effects.title: Adobe After Effects
adobe-after-effects.bestMethod: Use a Windows VM with GPU passthrough, or move motion-graphics work to Blender / Natron on Linux.
whatBreaks: No Linux build and no working Wine path
AEP projects and the plugin ecosystem do not transfer
GPU-accelerated effects require Windows/macOS
adobe-after-effects.notes: One of the least Linux-friendly Adobe apps; Blender covers some motion-graphics needs but is not a drop-in.

adobe-acrobat-pro.title: Adobe Acrobat Pro
adobe-acrobat-pro.bestMethod: Use Acrobat on the web for signing/editing, or native Linux PDF tools (Okular, Xournal++, Master PDF Editor) for most tasks.
whatBreaks: No Linux Acrobat desktop app (the old Reader for Linux is long discontinued)
Advanced PDF forms (XFA) and some e-sign workflows need Acrobat web
Preflight/print-production features have no free Linux equivalent
adobe-acrobat-pro.notes: Everyday PDF view/annotate/sign is well covered; pro print-production is the gap.

affinity-designer.title: Affinity Designer
affinity-designer.bestMethod: Same community ElementalWarrior Wine script as Affinity Photo; otherwise use Inkscape for native vector work.
whatBreaks: No official Linux build
Patched Wine setup required and may break on updates
Some export/PDF and GPU features are unreliable
affinity-designer.notes: Runs through the same community Wine workflow as the rest of the Affinity v2 suite.

affinity-publisher.title: Affinity Publisher
affinity-publisher.bestMethod: Use the community ElementalWarrior Wine script, or Scribus for native desktop publishing.
whatBreaks: No official Linux support
Patched Wine build needed; fragile across updates
Complex DTP features and font handling can misbehave under Wine
affinity-publisher.notes: Publisher is the heaviest of the Affinity apps under Wine; Scribus is the native fallback.

solidworks.title: SolidWorks
solidworks.bestMethod: Use a Windows VM (ideally with GPU passthrough), or evaluate the cloud 3DEXPERIENCE SolidWorks; FreeCAD for native open-source CAD.
whatBreaks: No Linux support and Wine does not run it
Requires certified GPU drivers, so VMs need passthrough for performance
SLDPRT/SLDASM files do not open natively in FreeCAD
solidworks.notes: Professional MCAD is one of the strongest reasons engineers keep Windows; Onshape (web) is the most Linux-friendly alternative.

fusion-360.title: Autodesk Fusion 360
fusion-360.bestMethod: Run Fusion 360 in a Windows VM, or via the community Wine/Bottles install (works for some); FreeCAD for native parametric CAD.
whatBreaks: No official Linux build
Community Wine installs break on Autodesk updates and lack GPU acceleration
Cloud-only data model adds friction
Simulation/CAM features may not run under Wine
fusion-360.notes: Some hobbyists run Fusion via Bottles, but it is unsupported and unreliable; a VM is the dependable path.

sketchup.title: SketchUp
sketchup.bestMethod: Use SketchUp for Web (free/paid tiers) in a browser; FreeCAD or Blender for native 3D modeling.
whatBreaks: No Linux desktop version
Web version lacks some pro extensions and the Ruby plugin ecosystem
Large models can be sluggish in the browser
sketchup.notes: SketchUp for Web covers most casual/architectural modeling on Linux.

quicken.title: Quicken
quicken.bestMethod: Use Quicken Simplifi (web) for new workflows, a Windows VM for Quicken Classic, or migrate to GnuCash.
whatBreaks: No Linux desktop version
Quicken data files (.QDF) do not import into GnuCash directly
Bank-connection/Quicken Bill features need the Windows app
quicken.notes: GnuCash/HomeBank are capable personal-finance replacements; legacy QDF data is the migration hurdle.

turbotax.title: TurboTax
turbotax.bestMethod: Use TurboTax Online in a browser; the desktop edition needs a Windows VM.
whatBreaks: No Linux desktop version
TurboTax Desktop (with extra forms/states) requires Windows/Mac or a VM
Some imports rely on Windows-only file formats
turbotax.notes: Online tax filing is fully usable on Linux; only the desktop edition is a problem.

sage.title: Sage Accounting
sage.bestMethod: Use Sage Business Cloud (web) where available; desktop Sage 50 requires a Windows VM.
whatBreaks: No Linux desktop version
Sage 50 desktop data needs Windows or a cloud migration
Some regional Sage products are web-only or Windows-only
sage.notes: Cloud Sage works on Linux; the desktop line does not.

vscode.title: Visual Studio Code
vscode.bestMethod: Install the official VS Code Linux build (deb/rpm from Microsoft's repo, or Flathub com.visualstudio.code).
whatBreaks: The Flatpak runs sandboxed, so toolchains need flatpak-spawn or the deb build for full host access
A few proprietary Microsoft extensions (e.g. C# Dev Kit, Live Share) can need extra setup
vscode.notes: First-class Linux support; identical to the Windows experience.

itunes.title: iTunes / Apple Music
itunes.bestMethod: Use Apple Music on the web (music.apple.com); for iTunes library/device sync use a Windows VM.
whatBreaks: No Linux version of iTunes/Apple Music
iPhone/iPod sync and backups need Windows/macOS
Wine installs of iTunes are unreliable on current versions
itunes.notes: Streaming is fine via web/Cider; device sync remains the hard part.

winrar.title: WinRAR
winrar.bestMethod: Use native archive tools (Ark, File Roller, PeaZip) with the rar/unrar CLI; WinRAR's GUI itself only runs under Wine.
whatBreaks: No native WinRAR GUI on Linux
Creating RAR archives needs the proprietary 'rar' binary (unrar handles extraction)
Windows shell-integration features are absent
winrar.notes: RarLab ships a Linux 'rar' CLI; GUI users just adopt Ark/PeaZip.

seven-zip.title: 7-Zip
seven-zip.bestMethod: Install p7zip / the official 7-Zip Linux CLI (7zz), with Ark or PeaZip as a GUI front end.
whatBreaks: The classic Windows 7-Zip File Manager GUI is not native (use Ark/PeaZip instead)
Windows Explorer shell/context-menu integration is absent
seven-zip.notes: 7-Zip now ships an official Linux CLI (7zz); p7zip and GUI front-ends cover everything else.

steam.title: Steam
steam.bestMethod: Install Steam natively and enable Steam Play (Proton) to run most Windows games; check ProtonDB before relying on a specific title.
whatBreaks: Some games with kernel-level anti-cheat (e.g. certain titles using Vanguard/BattlEye configs) do not run
A handful of DRM/launcher-wrapped games are unsupported
Day-one releases may need a newer Proton/GE build
steam.notes: Steam runs natively on Linux; Proton makes the large majority of Windows games playable, with kernel-level anti-cheat the main remaining blocker.

fortnite.title: Fortnite
fortnite.bestMethod: No supported path on Linux; Epic refuses to enable EAC for Proton, so use Windows, console, or cloud streaming (GeForce NOW/Xbox Cloud).
fortnite.warningNote: Epic has repeatedly declined to enable EasyAntiCheat on Linux/Steam Deck; the game is not on Steam and will not run via Proton online.

apex-legends.title: Apex Legends
apex-legends.bestMethod: There is no working method; EA blocks Linux entirely, so play on a Windows PC or console.
apex-legends.warningNote: EA disabled EasyAntiCheat support for Linux/Steam Deck in late 2024; launching on Linux now bans or blocks you from online matches.

valorant.title: Valorant
valorant.bestMethod: There is no Linux path; Vanguard is kernel-level and Riot blocks Proton, so use a Windows PC.
valorant.warningNote: Riot Vanguard is a kernel-level anti-cheat that refuses to run under Wine/Proton; Riot has publicly confirmed no Linux support.

league-of-legends.title: League of Legends
league-of-legends.bestMethod: No working Linux method since Vanguard was added; use a Windows PC.
league-of-legends.warningNote: Riot added kernel-level Vanguard to League in 2024, which blocks Wine/Proton; previously-working setups no longer launch.

call-of-duty.title: Call of Duty: Black Ops 6
call-of-duty.bestMethod: No Linux path for multiplayer; use Windows. Recent titles also require TPM 2.0 and Secure Boot.
call-of-duty.warningNote: RICOCHET kernel anti-cheat blocks Proton, and newer CoD titles enforce TPM 2.0/Secure Boot, making Linux play impossible.

pubg-battlegrounds.title: PUBG: Battlegrounds
pubg-battlegrounds.bestMethod: No working Linux path; the developer has not enabled BattlEye's Proton support, so use Windows.
pubg-battlegrounds.warningNote: BattlEye supports Proton, but PUBG's developers have not opted in, so the game fails to launch on Linux/Steam Deck.

grand-theft-auto-v.title: Grand Theft Auto V
grand-theft-auto-v.bestMethod: Story Mode plays great via Proton; for GTA Online expect failures and use Windows instead.
grand-theft-auto-v.warningNote: BattlEye was added to GTA Online in Sept 2024 and breaks online play on Linux; single-player Story Mode is unaffected.

ea-sports-fc.title: EA Sports FC 25
ea-sports-fc.bestMethod: No working Linux path; EA's kernel anti-cheat blocks Proton, so use Windows or console.
ea-sports-fc.warningNote: EA's Javelin/EA anti-cheat is kernel-level and is not enabled for Proton, so the game does not run on Linux/Steam Deck.

destiny-2.title: Destiny 2
destiny-2.bestMethod: No Linux path; Bungie deliberately did not enable BattlEye Proton support and bans Linux play, so use Windows.
destiny-2.warningNote: Bungie chose not to opt into BattlEye's Proton support; running Destiny 2 on Linux risks an account ban.

rust.title: Rust
rust.bestMethod: No working Linux path; Facepunch does not enable EAC for Proton and treats Linux as a cheat vector, so use Windows.
rust.warningNote: Facepunch dropped Linux/Proton EAC support; the COO stated there are no plans to re-enable it, so online play is blocked.

rainbow-six-siege.title: Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege
rainbow-six-siege.bestMethod: No working Linux path; Ubisoft has not enabled BattlEye's Proton support, so use Windows.
rainbow-six-siege.warningNote: BattlEye supports Proton, but Ubisoft has not opted in, so the game kicks Linux players from multiplayer.

roblox.title: Roblox
roblox.bestMethod: No supported Linux path since the Hyperion anti-cheat update; use Windows, ChromeOS, or mobile.
roblox.warningNote: Roblox's Hyperion (Byfron) anti-cheat blocks Wine/Proton since 2024; Sober and similar workarounds are unofficial and frequently break.

marvel-rivals.title: Marvel Rivals
marvel-rivals.bestMethod: Playable on desktop Linux with a recent Proton; add 'SteamDeck=1 %command%' to launch options if it refuses to start.
marvel-rivals.warningNote: NetEase's NEAC anti-cheat runs in user space and tolerates Proton, but it is officially unsupported and status is volatile across patches.

counter-strike-2.title: Counter-Strike 2
counter-strike-2.bestMethod: Just install and play; CS2 ships a native Linux build and VAC works fine.

elden-ring.title: Elden Ring
elden-ring.bestMethod: Install and play with the default Proton; EasyAntiCheat is Proton-enabled and online play works.

baldurs-gate-3.title: Baldur's Gate 3
baldurs-gate-3.bestMethod: Install and play; it runs great via Proton on desktop and has a native build path on Steam Deck.

cyberpunk-2077.title: Cyberpunk 2077
cyberpunk-2077.bestMethod: Install and play with the default Proton; no anti-cheat and excellent compatibility.

dead-by-daylight.title: Dead by Daylight
dead-by-daylight.bestMethod: Use the Steam version with default Proton; the developer enabled EasyAntiCheat for Proton.
dead-by-daylight.warningNote: Only the Steam build works; the Epic Games Store version of EAC is not Proton-enabled.

helldivers-2.title: Helldivers 2
helldivers-2.bestMethod: Install and play with a recent Proton (Proton-GE or Experimental helps); nProtect GameGuard runs in user space and works.
helldivers-2.warningNote: nProtect GameGuard works on Linux but has no kernel access; occasional patches can temporarily break compatibility.

dota-2.title: Dota 2
dota-2.bestMethod: Just install and play; Dota 2 has a native Linux build and VAC works.

team-fortress-2.title: Team Fortress 2
team-fortress-2.bestMethod: Just install and play; TF2 has a native Linux build and VAC works.

genshin-impact.title: Genshin Impact
genshin-impact.bestMethod: No safe Linux path: HoYoverse’s kernel-level anti-cheat (mhyprot) is not supported on Linux and playing via Wine risks a ban. Use Windows, console, or mobile.
genshin-impact.warningNote: Running Genshin Impact on Linux through Wine or unofficial launchers can get your HoYoverse account banned.

minecraft.title: Minecraft (Java Edition)
minecraft.bestMethod: Minecraft Java Edition runs natively on Linux via the official launcher (or Prism/MultiMC). Bedrock Edition has no native Linux build.
